title: Surprised doctors find 10-inch worm in man's groin during elective surgery
gist: An NEJM case report describes surgeons finding a live 26 cm Spirometra tapeworm in a 71-year-old man's abdomen during hernia surgery, his second such discovery, likely from eating raw snake meat 50 years earlier.

insights.claim: Surgeons repairing an inguinal hernia found a live 26 cm (10.2 inch) Spirometra erinaceieuropaei tapeworm wedged between the man's bladder and pubic bone, identified via genetic test as causing sparganosis.
insights.speaker: Beth Mole
insights.so_what: A rare human sparganosis case documented in NEJM; primarily of clinical curiosity value.
insights.locator: As they worked, they noticed something whitish

insights.claim: The infection was clinically silent: the patient had no symptoms, normal blood work, and no elevated white blood cell count that would signal a parasitic infection.
insights.speaker: Beth Mole
insights.so_what: Highlights that these migrating parasites can evade standard diagnostic markers, a diagnostic-blind-spot note for clinicians.
insights.locator: Surgeons noted that the man appeared in good health

insights.claim: This was the man's second worm; four years earlier a 18 cm worm was found during a left-side hernia surgery but went unidentified and untreated, and Spirometra can live 20-30 years in humans.
insights.speaker: Beth Mole
insights.so_what: The long lifespan and repeat finding underscore how persistent and under-diagnosed such infections can be.

insights.claim: Humans disrupt the parasite's normal dog/cat lifecycle by drinking contaminated water or eating undercooked fish, reptiles, or amphibians; the patient's likely exposure was raw snake meat eaten during military service 50 years prior.
insights.speaker: Beth Mole
insights.so_what: The decades-long latency from a single raw-meat exposure is the notable epidemiological takeaway.
